admin.desc: the guarded admin surface: every route requires a valid admin bearer token (env-backed, constant-time compared, non-enumerable) — deny-by-default · an unauthorized request is 401 and records NOTHING (no trace from a failed attempt) · authorized actions are recorded APPEND-ONLY with a monotonic id (no update or delete route exists — the admin trail is immutable by construction) · the admin-only list is bounded through the shared paginate part (bounded page + opaque cursor) · self-contained: admin records action intents over a target reference, it never imports a sibling domain (the boundaries law) · durable store seam · strict input validation in all three languages

agent.desc: AI agent runtime (package shape): swappable provider port (AI_PROVIDER, fake = deterministic default; SHIPPED stdlib adapters for anthropic + openai — set the matching key env and every run round-trips the real API, upstream failures mapping to a loud 502/504 problem+json with a sanitized detail; a keyless real name or an unknown name fails LOUD per run — 501 naming exactly what to set — never silent fake output) · tool registry (safe calc, never eval) · durable agents/sessions/memory · bounded run loop that ALWAYS terminates

ai_memory.desc: ai_memory — a long-term, owner-scoped agent-memory store whose dangerous property is RETENTION-ENFORCED / BOUNDED: a memory past its retention (TTL-expired, cap-evicted, or explicitly forgotten) is DETERMINISTICALLY not retrievable, and an owner's store can NEVER grow unbounded. This is a DELIBERATE IMPROVEMENT over the field (mem0/Letta-archival/Chroma/AutoGen mostly grow-forever + explicit-delete-only): ai_memory ENFORCES retention, offline + identical x3. (1) BOUNDED (the headline): growth is bounded by MAX_SCOPES x MAX_MEMORIES per owner. A per-(owner,scope) index caps memories per scope (evict past AI_MEMORY_MAX_MEMORIES); a per-owner scope index caps the NUMBER of scopes (a NEW scope past AI_MEMORY_MAX_SCOPES is REJECTED 422, never a silent success) — because a per-scope cap ALONE leaves the per-owner total unbounded when `scope` is a free-form caller string (the partition-COUNT trap). (2) EVICTION: when a scope is full, evict the min-(importance ASC, created_at ASC, id ASC) memory, EXPIRED-FIRST — a deterministic superset of FIFO (default importance 0 => pure FIFO) that never drops a LIVE memory while an expired one keeps its slot; a high-importance mark-to-keep survives an older low one (age != staleness in a long-term store). (3) TTL: optional per-memory ttl_seconds => a SERVER-DERIVED expires_at (a smuggled expires_at is discarded; the sum is overflow-guarded + clamped to 2^53-1 so the derived int is identical x3); expiry is lazy read-hide with `expired <=> now > expires_at` (AT the boundary is LIVE), inert without APP_TEST_CLOCK. (4) FORGET: DELETE by id (purge the scope-index entry then the row) or DELETE a whole scope (scope REQUIRED — no silent wipe-all); a forgotten memory is 404 + list-excluded. (5) OWNER-SCOPED: owner = the authenticated subject (require_identity, never a body field); memory rows are keyed by the composite <owner>\x1f<id> (id server-minted via next_id, globally unique => by-id is O(1) and owner-scoped by construction; the \x1f separator is a control char well_formed rejects, so the key can't be forged) and a get/delete of another owner's memory is 404 (byte-indistinguishable from missing); the per-scope index is keyed <owner>\x1f<scope>. scope is a PARTITION, NOT a security boundary (only owner is). content/tags/metadata are contained (make_well_formed / sanitize_json — keys AND values) BEFORE store, so a lone surrogate anywhere is U+FFFD, never an uncontained 5xx on a later re-read. Retrieval is per-scope, newest-first (created_at desc, id asc), expired-excluded, paginated, with optional ?tag= and ?q= (ASCII-only case-fold substring — a deterministic FLOOR, honestly not 'world-class keyword search'); semantic relevance is an offline-OPTIONAL port (see INTEROP.md). Append-only: NO update route, NO dedup (recurrence is information for a log). The managed denominator (mem0/Zep/Letta/Vertex Memory Bank) converges on add/get/list/search/delete/delete_all; ours is the offline, deterministic, retention-ENFORCED carrier. Durable; strict input validation in all three languages. HONEST LIMITS: the BOUND is on the RETRIEVABLE + counted store (bounded MAX_SCOPES x MAX_MEMORIES, proven under a concurrent forget-scope+add race); a row orphaned by that rare race is non-retrievable + non-counted but physically reclaimed lazily (a v2 sweep). TTL-expiry is lazy read-hide: an expired memory is not retrievable but its row is NOT erased from storage until cap-eviction or an explicit forget purges it (parity with mem0 expiration_date) - for guaranteed erasure, forget explicitly. importance BIASES eviction (expired-first dominates it, and it cannot exceed the per-scope cap) - it is not an absolute pin. Append-only: no update route and no audit-of-forget/history trail (a v2 compliance layer).

ai_provider.desc: the unified LLM gateway — ONE seam every caller uses for completions: a deterministic OFFLINE fake (the test oracle) plus SHIPPED stdlib adapters for Anthropic + OpenAI behind the same shape (set AI_PROVIDER + the matching key env; one configured model per deployment via AI_MODEL; per-call timeout; upstream failures map to a loud 502/504 problem+json with a sanitized detail — never billed, never cached) · FAIL-LOUD provider honesty: AI_PROVIDER naming a real provider WITHOUT its key env, or any unknown value, refuses completions per call (501 naming exactly what to set; GET /ai/usage keeps working; a refusal is never billed or cached) — never silent fake output under a real provider's name · model FALLBACK (an unknown model degrades to the default, never a 5xx) · response CACHING (an identical model+prompt replays the stored answer and is NOT re-billed; the cache key is the digest part, never a joined string) · METERING that is CONSERVED: GET usage equals the sum of every billed completion, the meter update is one atomic read-modify-write through the do seam · GET /ai/usage is ADMIN-ONLY (the core require_admin seam — no token is 401, a non-admin is 403, resolved BEFORE the read): it is the GLOBAL spend metric across ALL callers, so the app's total AI spend can't leak to an anonymous or unprivileged caller · POST /ai/complete is authenticated (any caller) · token counts are utf-8 BYTE lengths (the ×3-identical semantic) · durable store seam · strict input validation in all three languages

ai_tools.desc: the typed tool belt — a registry of tools any caller (an agent, a workflow) invokes over HTTP. Each tool declares a TYPED CONTRACT: a description + an input_schema (JSON Schema — per-arg type, and which args are required), so a caller or a model knows the shape before it calls · the args are VALIDATED against it — a MISSING required arg, or an arg of the WRONG TYPE (integer args are strict: 5.0, "5" and true are rejected identically in all three languages), returns a CONTAINED result (ok:false + the error, HTTP 200 — never a crash, never a 5xx) · an unknown tool is 404 · an undeclared extra arg is ignored · every tool is deterministic and BOUNDED (repeat is capped — output can never explode) · string ops work by CODEPOINTS in all three languages · the tool catalog (GET /tools) is a deliberately PUBLIC function-signature listing that exposes no secret · the result is a flat string for these string tools · the registry is static policy (no store); new tools drop in behind the same typed invoke contract

ai_workflow.desc: multi-step pipelines over a running value: an ordered step list where each step's output threads into the next · a run ALWAYS terminates (MAX_STEPS bounds every run — no runaway pipeline) · a failing or unknown step is CONTAINED (the run stops gracefully with ok:false and the trace so far — never a crash, never a 5xx) · deterministic: string ops slice/measure by CODEPOINTS in all three languages · definitions are durable · the step ops are simple transforms here; real steps (a gateway completion, a tool invoke) drop in behind the same contract · strict input validation in all three languages

api_keys.desc: API keys: issue/list/verify/rotate/revoke with scopes. The secret is shown ONCE at issue and stored only as a sha256 hash (digest part) — never plaintext at rest (a 192-bit random secret + a single approved one-way hash is exactly the look-up-secret storage rule; a slow KDF is for passwords, not high-entropy keys) · verify is CONSTANT-TIME and NON-ENUMERABLE: an unknown key id and a wrong secret are the same {valid:false} after the same work (no timing oracle, no existence leak), scopes return only when valid (scopes are ADVISORY — verify RETURNS them but enforces nothing; the consumer is the enforcer, they are not an authz boundary here) · LIST is OWNER-SCOPED + paginated: a caller sees only their own keys ({results, next_cursor}, never an unbounded dump), a stranger gets an empty page (never 403), and no list/get response ever carries the secret or owner · every key records created_at (seconds, from the core clock seam) · rotation mints a new secret and invalidates the old one (the credential is never unchanging) · revocation is MONOTONIC (a revoked key verifies false forever) · the public record never carries the secret_hash or owner · the management ops are user-scoped (a cross-owner key id is 404, byte-identical to a missing id) · durable store seam · strict input validation in all three languages

audit_log.desc: append-only, tamper-evident evidence log: every event records WHO (actor) · WHAT (action) · WHEN (at — seconds since epoch, from the core clock seam) and is hash-chained over the COMPLETE record (sha256 of prev:id:at:H(actor):H(action), GENESIS-rooted — the two free-form fields are pre-hashed so the ':'-join stays injective and an actor/action containing ':' can't forge another record) · the append is ONE atomic read-modify-write on the chain head through the do seam, so two processes can never fork the chain · immutable by construction (no update/delete route exists) · /verify re-derives the whole chain and reports ANY damage loudly (a tampered action, a backdated timestamp, a forged actor, a missing event) · durable store seam · strict input validation in all three languages · WRITES ARE SERVICE-ONLY, THE DISCLOSING READ ADMIN-ONLY: POST /events (append) is gated by the trusted SERVICE seam (core.require_service) — audit events are ingested by app services on a user's behalf, not posted by end users — and GET /events (list) requires the 'admin' role (core require_admin); an anonymous append is log-poisoning (the chain stays 'valid' over forged rows, so the integrity proof can't catch it) and the list discloses every subject's events; a missing/wrong service token on the append is 401 (an admin session is a user, not a service — also 401), a non-admin on the list is 403, the append's body-only precedence is PARSE->AUTH->SEMANTIC (an unauthenticated ill-typed body is 401), identical x3; GET /verify stays OPEN — the integrity probe returns only {valid, count, detail}, no event contents, so anyone may ask 'is the chain intact?'

auth.desc: password authentication + the full session lifecycle, OWASP ASVS V2/V3-shaped and interop-ready (the session envelope matches Supabase/Firebase/Auth0/Clerk/Cognito; see INTEROP.md): salted PBKDF2 via the central password_hash part · constant-time verify · no enumeration · the core <id>.<secret> session seam with absolute TTL + rotation (/refresh) + scoped logout · enumeration-safe registration · single-use expiring password-reset and email-verify token flows · throttled pre-auth endpoints · durable store · identical in all three languages

billing.desc: subscription billing: the amount is DERIVED from a fixed plan catalog, never client-set (no over/under-charge by construction — the input model has no amount field) · unknown plan -> 422, deny-by-default · the owner is the AUTHENTICATED identity (the core require_identity seam) derived from the bearer token, NEVER a client-supplied field, so a caller only ever reads/cancels THEIR OWN subscriptions (another owner's subscription is 404, indistinguishable from missing) · deny-by-default (no token -> 401) · the lifecycle is MONOTONIC: active -> canceled, cancel is idempotent and a canceled subscription never returns to active · durable store seam · strict input validation in all three languages

chat_threads.desc: chat_threads — a durable, owner-scoped AI-chat history store: threads plus an APPEND-ONLY, seq-ORDERED, IMMUTABLE message log per thread, bounded on BOTH axes. (1) ORDERED: every message's position is a server-minted, per-thread strictly-monotonic seq, incremented inside the store's atomic read-modify-write seam on the thread row — two racing appends serialize to distinct consecutive seqs (no lost update), and the transcript reads back in exactly append order in all three languages (a replay that reorders turns silently corrupts a model's context). (2) IMMUTABLE: no route edits or deletes a single message — history is a record, not a cache ('edit' in a chat product is appending a new turn). (3) BOUNDED BOTH WAYS: messages per thread (CHAT_THREADS_MAX_MESSAGES) AND threads per owner (CHAT_THREADS_MAX_THREADS) are both capped with a loud 422 past the cap — NEVER silent eviction, because dropping a user's chat history is data loss (the caller frees space by deleting threads); the per-owner total is bounded by MAX_THREADS x MAX_MESSAGES by construction. (4) OWNER-ISOLATED: owner = the authenticated subject (require_identity, never a body field); thread rows are keyed by the composite <owner>\x1f<id> and message rows by <owner>\x1f<id>\x1f<seq> (ids and seqs are server-minted digits and the owner is the trusted token subject, so the key cannot be forged by any client string), and every surface answers 404 for another owner's thread — existence never leaks. The per-owner thread index is the LIVENESS authority: every per-thread surface gates on it, so a deleted thread's residue is never resurrected. (5) CASCADE DELETE: deleting a thread frees its cap slot FIRST (it is instantly non-listable), then removes the thread row and reaps its message rows — unlike providers that leave a deleted conversation's items retrievable. last_seq is the honest high-water mark of ACCEPTED appends (after a crash between the seq mint and the message write the transcript can be shorter — never reordered). Message content is free text, contained before store (a lone surrogate becomes U+FFFD, never a failed re-read) and byte-capped; the title is a display line (control characters rejected); metadata keys AND values are contained and bounded (16 pairs, 64-char keys, 512-char values — the field's settled numbers). The role set is CLOSED (user|assistant|system|tool, exact lowercase — a case-fold would drift across languages). Retrieval: threads list newest-activity-first (an append bumps updated_at, so an active thread rises to the head); messages list in seq order (the replay order); both paginated. The resource shape follows the settled thread+message model (a stateful conversation of immutable turns); generating an assistant turn is the agent seam's job — this domain PERSISTS turns, it never calls a model (see INTEROP.md for the replay projections per provider). Durable; strict input validation in all three languages. HONEST LIMITS: caps reject (never evict); content is a single text string (structured content parts are a v2); turn alternation is not enforced (a provider-replay concern); a crash between the index step and the row step leaves benign residue on the SAFE side only (a ghost cap slot, a seq gap, or an unreachable orphan row — reclaimed lazily).

crew.desc: multi-agent orchestration: a crew of named roles where each role processes the running value and hands off to the next · the load-bearing invariant: a run ALWAYS terminates even when the handoff graph CYCLES (A->B->A) — MAX_HANDOFFS bounds every run, no infinite ping-pong · handoffs THREAD (each role builds on the prior output) · a handoff to an UNKNOWN role is CONTAINED (the run stops gracefully with terminated:false and the trace so far — never a crash) · deterministic · role names are unique, well-formed identifiers · definitions are durable · roles are tagged contributions here; real roles (an agent with a provider + tools) drop in behind the same handoff contract · strict input validation in all three languages

email_outbox.desc: outbound email behind a provider PORT (offline default: the message is RECORDED to the store — the record IS the outbox; a real smtp/sendgrid/ses backend is the documented swap-point). COST of a mistake is a double-send or a header injection, so two dangerous properties are proven · EXACTLY-ONCE DISPATCH: sending is idempotent on the Idempotency-Key via the scoped_key + claim_once seam (two processes racing one key produce ONE recorded message, the loser is served the winner); the slot is OWNER-scoped so a key is PRIVATE to its caller (no cross-tenant replay/grief), and a same-key retry with ANY different message (recipient/subject/body/template) is 409 — never a silent re-send, never a dropped Bcc · HEADER SAFETY: every header-bound field rejects CR/LF + control/NEL/line-separator — addresses via a strict validator, the subject via a header-text validator run AFTER template rendering (a template variable cannot inject a second header line); we REJECT (422), deliberately stricter than libraries that silently sanitize · OWNER-SCOPED: owner = the authenticated subject (never a body field) — listing or reading another caller's message is 404, byte-indistinguishable from missing · APPEND-ONLY (no update/delete route) · a small code-reviewed TEMPLATE registry (id + string->string data, name-lookup substitution) · recipients are address-validated, de-duplicated across to/cc/bcc, and count-capped; the rendered body is UTF-8-octet-capped, the subject length-capped · durable (a keyed send dedups after a restart) · every route require_identity (no token 401) · strict input validation in all three languages

evals.desc: evals: a deterministic, OFFLINE scoring harness for model outputs. Store an IMMUTABLE owner-scoped golden SUITE (a named set of cases, each {id, scorer, expected}), then SCORE caller-PROVIDED outputs against it - evals never calls a model; it scores the text you give it. The dangerous property is SCORE-SOUNDNESS: the verdict is SERVER-DERIVED over a FROZEN suite (a client cannot forge a pass), and DETERMINISTIC - score(scorer, output, expected) is a pure function whose per-case pass is byte-identical across python, go and node and reproducible across runs and restart. Owner-scoped two ways: the suite store key is the composite <owner>\x1f<name> (caller B can never clobber caller A's suite name - the separator is a control character that identifiers reject, so the key cannot be forged), and every read filters on the authenticated owner (not-yours is 404, existence never leaks). The owner is taken from the token, never a request field. A suite is IMMUTABLE on create (a second create of the same name is 409, via the atomic single-key claim seam), so a scored run is reproducible against a frozen golden set. Seven scorers, proven byte-identical across all three languages: exact / contains / starts_with / ends_with (raw code-point), iexact / icontains (ASCII case-fold A-Z<->a-z, non-ASCII compared as-is), equals_int (a canonical integer in the JS-safe range). Scoring is STATELESS (it returns a verdict, storing nothing); the pass result and integer counts are exact, never a float. Regex, float-similarity scores (BLEU/ROUGE/cosine), structural JSON equality, and full Unicode case folding are deliberate v2 additions behind the same routes - each is a genuine cross-language non-determinism, not a gap. The real judge / rubric / generated-output harness swaps in behind these routes.

feature_flags.desc: feature flags with deterministic percentage rollout via stable hash bucketing: a flag key is claimed once (duplicate 409) · each subject's bucket is a fixed function of (key, subject) through the digest part, so evaluation is DETERMINISTIC (same answer every time) and MONOTONIC under rollout increase (raising the percentage only ever ADDS subjects — a subject already enabled never flips off, no flapping during a ramp) · rollout is an integer 0..100 (0 = nobody, 100 = everybody) · unknown flags are 404 · durable store seam · strict input validation in all three languages · WRITES ARE ADMIN-ONLY: a feature flag is a control-plane kill-switch, so create (POST) and set-rollout (PUT) require the 'admin' role (the core require_admin seam) — no token 401, a non-admin 403, resolved BEFORE any body/path validation (×3); the READS stay OPEN on purpose — GET a flag and GET .../evaluate are the runtime hot path consuming apps call per request, so evaluate MUST NOT be admin

file_store.desc: file_store — a durable, owner-scoped store of REAL BYTE objects behind a swappable provider port (the storage IDIOM, its own composite-key store). Upload is base64-in-JSON (the GitHub Contents denominator): POST a {key, content_b64, content_type?} body; the server decodes with the CANONICAL-ROUND-TRIP base64 rule (pre-check the alphabet + length, decode, re-encode, require equality) so the accept set is BYTE-IDENTICAL in all three languages (a non-canonical b64 is 422 identically). Download is REAL RAW BYTES: GET returns the exact stored bytes with the stored Content-Type reflected, a content-addressed quoted ETag, and the stored-XSS defense headers X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff + Content-Disposition: attachment (a VALID text/html served same-origin is the attack the write grammar cannot block), plus an explicit Content-Length. GET /{key}/meta is the JSON mirror; GET / is a paginated {key,size} listing from the per-owner index; DELETE frees the quota slot. OWNER-ISOLATED: owner is the authenticated subject (require_identity, never a body field), the object row is keyed by the composite <owner>\x1f<key> (the key is grammar-checked so the \x1f separator cannot be forged), and a cross-owner get/meta/delete is 404 byte-indistinguishable from missing. BOUNDED BOTH WAYS: a per-owner file-COUNT cap AND a per-owner total-BYTES quota, enforced PER ADMISSION inside ONE atomic index do() (the old size read from the entry INSIDE the fn), reject-past-cap with a loud 422 - never silent eviction. HEADER-BOUND content_type: the reflected type is allowlist-validated at WRITE against a strict RFC-2045 token grammar (CR/LF/controls structurally impossible). KEY GRAMMAR: make_well_formed at ingress (a lone surrogate becomes U+FFFD), then non-empty, no control chars, <=1024 utf-8 bytes, and no '/' '\\' '.' or '..' (an un-addressable / un-freeable quota slot is the bug this closes). The decoded bytes are opaque (never utf-8-decoded, hashed as text, or logged). The provider is selected ONCE (FILE_STORE_PROVIDER): 'store' = the durable runtime seam (default + deterministic oracle), 's3' = the fail-loud customization stub. Durable; strict input validation in all three languages. HONEST LIMITS: there is no GLOBAL storage cap (the owner space is bounded only by the auth domain's signup controls); the byte-quota + count-cap are per-owner; a crash between the index admission and the row write leaves benign residue on the SAFE side only (a phantom over-reserves; the drift is bounded by MAX_BYTES per key and self-heals on the next clean write). Raw-PUT / multipart upload is a v2 behind a documented runtime seam - v1 is base64-in-JSON up, raw bytes down, with zero runtime change.

idempotency.desc: replay-safe writes per the IETF Idempotency-Key shape: same key + same body -> the STORED response replayed, the side effect runs EXACTLY ONCE (the claim is a single atomic read-modify-write through the do seam — two processes racing the same key produce one winner, never a double charge) · same key + different body -> 409 · no key -> processed normally (idempotency is opt-in) · the Idempotency-Key is a DEDUPE token, NOT identity — every mutating route ALSO requires an AUTHENTICATED caller via the core require_identity seam (deny-by-default, no token -> 401) · the dedup slot is SCOPED TO THAT CALLER (collision-safe — a key is private to its caller; caller B can never replay nor 409-block caller A's slot) · durable store seam (a replay works across a restart) · keys are DURABLE BY DESIGN — they never expire, so exactly-once holds forever (the safe default for money: no replay-after-expiry double-charge); a deployment that needs storage bounds adds an infra-layer reaper · strict input validation in all three languages

invitations.desc: invite + accept flow with single-use, expiring tokens: creating an invite mints an unguessable token (server-side random, never client-set) with an expiry derived from the test-clock seam · accepting is SINGLE-USE and atomic (the consume is one read-modify-write through the do seam — two processes racing one token yield one acceptance and one 409) · an expired token is 410 even if never used (expiry beats availability) · an unknown token is 404 · the token is unpredictable so the create->accept flow is proven in the invariant test; the manifest pins the deterministic create fields · durable store seam · strict input validation in all three languages

invoices.desc: invoices — a CONSERVED multi-line bill (create-draft + retrieve). POST /invoices creates a DRAFT: each line item carries description + quantity + unit_amount (positive integers in minor units), and the server RECOMPUTES the money — line amount = unit_amount × quantity, subtotal = Σ line amounts, total = subtotal + tax — DISCARDING any client-supplied total (the conservation law: a stored total always reconciles to its lines + tax). Every amount is a strict integer CAPPED at 2^53-1, the cross-language-safe ceiling: this domain SUMS many line items, so the per-line PRODUCT and the running subtotal/total are each bounded BEFORE they can wrap Go's int64 / lose Node's float precision (the multiply overflows first — checked by division, identical ×3). The line_items list is COUNT-bounded (a single record's list can't grow without limit). OWNER-SCOPED: a bill belongs to its creating caller (the core require_identity seam) — every route is deny-by-default authenticated (no token -> 401), the bill is addressed by (caller, id) so a cross-caller get is 404 (byte-indistinguishable from missing — existence never leaks), and the list returns ONLY the caller's own bills (owner-FIELD-filtered, bounded by the shared paginate part). The id is DERIVED: id = digest.scoped_key('POST /invoices', caller, Idempotency-Key) — a deterministic, caller-private, idempotent slot, so re-creating the SAME key+body returns the SAME draft (exactly-once create via the do seam, no double-create under racing processes), while the same key with a DIFFERENT body is a 409. The finalize (assign the legal number, freeze) + pay/void/uncollectible transitions + their conservation/immutability invariants land in subsequent waves. Durable across restart; identical ×3.

job_queue.desc: jobs: an async job QUEUE — enqueue a unit of background work, a trusted worker pool claims the next ready job under an exclusive lease, then completes or fails it; a failed job retries with deterministic backoff until it is dead-lettered. At-most-once claim: a ready job is leased to at most one worker at a time (a single-key compare-and-swap, so two workers racing the same job cannot both win); the pick is the lowest-id ready job. Completion-auth: claim mints a rotating lease token, and complete/fail require it (the compare-and-swap asserts the token is current and the job is running), so a stale worker whose lease expired and whose job was reclaimed cannot complete or reset the new claimant's job. Bounded retry: a job is delivered at most max_attempts times whether the failure is explicit or a crash (the lease lapses and the job is reclaimed), because attempts increments at claim and both the fail path and the reclaim path dead-letter at the bound. Deterministic backoff: on each fail run_at advances by min(base * 2^attempts, cap) with no jitter, identical across all three languages. Owner-scoped: enqueue stamps the owner from the authenticated subject (never a body field), get and list return only the caller's jobs and a cross-owner id is 404 (existence never leaks); the worker pool (claim/complete/fail) is the trusted service seam, cross-owner infrastructure. The opaque payload is contained for safe durable storage (a lone surrogate becomes U+FFFD, integers are bounded to the safe range). At-least-once delivery (handlers must be idempotent) is the universal queue contract; the local store-backed implementation is the deterministic oracle, with INTEROP.md mapping the envelope onto SQS, River, BullMQ and Sidekiq. Bounded opaque-cursor pagination; the RFC 9457 problem+json error envelope; strict input validation in all three languages.

ledger.desc: double-entry ledger: every tx balances (sum == 0, >= 2 entries, else 422) · append-only/immutable · balances DERIVED never stored · ADMIN-ONLY: both routes require the 'admin' role (the core require_admin seam) — financial infra with no per-account owner, so no token is 401 and a non-admin is 403, resolved BEFORE any body/path validation (an unauthenticated ill-typed body is 401, never a 422 that leaks shape) · durable store seam · strict input validation in all three languages

llm_usage.desc: a per-call LLM token + cost METER — the AI spend meter feeding billing. COST INTEGRITY by construction: the cost is SERVER-DERIVED from a fixed code-reviewed price table — the event carries TOKEN COUNTS only (no cost field, so a client cannot smuggle one); an unknown (provider, model) or an unpriced token dimension is 422, deny-by-default (never a silent $0/free, never an under-count) · NO DOUBLE-COUNT: recording is idempotent on (owner, identifier) via the scoped_key + claim_once seam (two processes racing one identifier produce ONE event, the loser gets the winner); the slot is OWNER-scoped so an identifier is private to its caller (no cross-tenant replay/grief), and a same-identifier retry with ANY different cost-input is 409 (the provider-inclusive body fingerprint, computed over the request AS SENT — an omitted `at` hashes as a sentinel, never the server-minted default, so a byte-identical retry replays 201 even across a wall-clock second tick — no silent re-bill) · APPEND-ONLY: events are immutable (no update/delete route) · the aggregate is DERIVED on read (GET /summary, owner-scoped, grouped by provider+model), never stored · OWNER-SCOPED: owner = the authenticated subject (the require_identity seam), NOT a client field — summary and list count ONLY the caller's own events · INTEGER-EXACT, OVERFLOW-SAFE: tokens and cost are SafeInt nanodollars, the per-token rate is integer nanodollars-per-1000-tokens (real per-token rates are sub-nanodollar) and cost = tokens × rate / 1000 with no float anywhere, a per-dimension token ceiling rejects an absurd count · the client event time is validated within a replay window (anti-backdate) · every route require_identity (no token 401) · durable store seam · strict input validation in all three languages

notifications.desc: in-app notifications: SENDING is authenticated (the core require_identity seam) and the notification's `from` is STAMPED from the authenticated subject — never a caller-supplied body field, so the sender cannot be forged (no token -> 401) · recipient-scoped to the AUTHENTICATED identity (NOT a caller-supplied param): listing or acting as a different recipient never sees or touches a notification (cross-recipient access -> 404, indistinguishable from missing — no existence leak), and a request without a valid bearer token is 401 · the read-state is MONOTONIC: unread -> read, marking read is idempotent (a terminal-value write — concurrent marks converge), a read notification never returns to unread · durable store seam (the read-state survives a restart) · strict input validation in all three languages

oauth.desc: OAuth 2.0 authorization-code flow (server side): the callback state must match a PENDING flow issued FOR THAT PROVIDER (forged/unknown -> 403, the CSRF defense) · the code is SINGLE-USE — the consume is one atomic read-modify-write through the do seam, so two processes racing a code produce one token and one 409 · a state is single-use END TO END: re-authorizing a consumed state is 409, never a silent resurrection (a naive implementation would re-open consumed flows for replay) · only configured providers, deny-by-default · the access token is an UNGUESSABLE server-minted CSPRNG value bound to the flow on consume (never a forgeable digest of the client-supplied inputs; a real deployment exchanges the code at the provider's token endpoint) · durable store seam · strict input validation in all three languages

orgs.desc: organizations / workspaces, the ownership root tenancy and teams hang off, with MULTI-MEMBER roles: a slug is claimed EXACTLY ONCE (atomic via idempotent_claim; duplicate is 409) · an org is NEVER OWNERLESS — created with an owner STAMPED FROM THE AUTHENTICATED TOKEN (never a body field), ownership TRANSFERS only to a well-formed handle and there is EXACTLY ONE owner at all times (the owner can never be removed/demoted — transfer is the only move, demoting the old owner to admin) · archival is MONOTONIC and idempotent · ROLE-GOVERNED MEMBERSHIP, PENDING UNTIL ACCEPTED, in the cross-cutting orgs_members store (key <slug>\x1f<handle> -> a self-describing record {org, handle, role, status}, the store the core org_role seam reads — a role is conferred ONLY when status==active) — add/set/remove member + archive require the caller's org_role owner|admin, a manager may grant only admin|member (ownership moves only via transfer), a member can never escalate itself, a non-member is 403; add_member is an INVITE that writes a PENDING row + mints a single-use token (delivered to the orgs_outbox), and the role is granted only once the INVITED party ACCEPTs with that token via POST /orgs/{slug}/members/accept — so pre-naming a raw handle a manager does not control grants nothing (the member-identity escalation fix) · every mutation is deny-by-default authenticated (no token -> 401), authz resolved BEFORE body validation (non-member -> 403 not 422) · cross-org reads of a missing slug are an honest 404 · LISTING — list-my-orgs (GET /orgs, the caller's owned/active-member orgs), the member roster (GET /orgs/{slug}/members, MEMBER-scoped, the DERIVED owner + every ACTIVE member, a non-member 404 byte-identical to missing), and pending invitations (GET /orgs/{slug}/invitations, owner|admin only, never the secret) — every collection read routed through the bounded paginate seam, returning {results, next_cursor} · SELF-LEAVE (POST /orgs/{slug}/leave) — the caller leaves (the owner cannot, 409, never ownerless; MEMBER-SCOPED, so a non-member is 404 byte-identical to a missing slug — existence never leaks and a non-member cannot pump no-op leave audits) · durable store seam · strict input validation in all three languages

payments.desc: payments — a provider-agnostic payment-INTENT lifecycle (authorize + retrieve). POST /payments AUTHORIZES an intent: amount is a positive integer in minor units CAPPED at 2^53-1 (the cross-language-safe ceiling — this domain SUMS balances across transitions, and an uncapped amount would wrap Go's int64 / lose Node's float precision = an over-refund that conserves on paper), currency is a CLOSED ISO-4217 code. OWNER-SCOPED: an intent belongs to its authorizing caller (the core require_identity seam) — every route is deny-by-default authenticated (no token -> 401), the intent is addressed by (caller, id) so a cross-caller get is 404 (byte-indistinguishable from missing — existence never leaks), and the list returns ONLY the caller's own intents (owner-FIELD-filtered, bounded by the shared paginate part). The intent id is DERIVED: id = digest.scoped_key('POST /payments', caller, Idempotency-Key) — a deterministic, caller-private, idempotent slot, so re-authorizing the SAME key+body returns the SAME intent (exactly-once create via the do seam, no double-authorization under racing processes), while the same key with a DIFFERENT body is a 409. The capture/void/refund transitions + their conservation invariants land in subsequent waves. Durable across restart; identical ×3.

prompt_registry.desc: prompt_registry — a versioned, IMMUTABLE prompt-template store with movable deployment LABELS (rollback) and a deterministic {{variable}} render. The dangerous property is PIN-HONESTY: when a consumer asks for (version | label) it gets EXACTLY the bytes that were pinned, and a named label does NOT move on its own. (1) IMMUTABILITY: each POST mints a NEW monotonic version per (owner,name) through the do() seam; a published version's (template, content_hash) is frozen forever — the only way to change a prompt is to ADD a version; there is NO update/delete route (append-only). (2) LABEL NO-DRIFT: a label is a movable pointer to ONE version; setting it MOVES it (one-to-one); rolling it back to an older version always resolves to that version's EXACT immutable content; creating new versions NEVER moves an existing label (a production label that silently followed the newest version is the catastrophic prod-break this domain refuses — there is NO virtual 'latest', no silent default; render/get require an explicit {version|label}). (3) CONTENT PIN: content_hash = sha256 over the contained template (one pre-contained string -> injective by construction); SERVER-DERIVED (a smuggled content_hash is discarded). (4) DETERMINISTIC RENDER: render(version|label, data) substitutes {{var}} from a string->string data map — ASCII [A-Za-z0-9_] placeholders (NOT \w, which go/node diverge on for unicode), scan the TEMPLATE not the data (deterministic x3), SINGLE-PASS (a substituted value is never re-scanned, so a data value cannot inject a second variable and a self-referential {{x}}->{{x}} terminates), a missing variable is 422 (fail-loud, never a silent blank), the data is contained (a lone surrogate becomes U+FFFD, never an uncontained 5xx) then the rendered output is bounded (PROMPT_REGISTRY_MAX_RENDERED_BYTES, the amplification cap). (5) OWNER-SCOPED: owner = the authenticated subject (require_identity, never a body field); the store key is the composite <owner>\x1f<name> so caller B can NEVER clobber caller A's name (the \x1f separator is a control char well_formed rejects -> the key can't be forged), and a get/render/set-label/list of another caller's prompt is 404 (byte-indistinguishable from missing). Bounded: versions reject past PROMPT_REGISTRY_MAX_VERSIONS (preserve pins), labels reject past PROMPT_REGISTRY_MAX_LABELS, the template is byte-capped; reads paginate. Durable; strict input validation in all three languages. The managed prompt-registry denominator (Langfuse/PromptLayer/LangSmith Prompt Hub) defers render to the client SDK and serves the raw template (our get-version does too); our /render is the offline, deterministic, identical-x3 carrier (see INTEROP.md).

rag.desc: retrieval pipeline (chunk -> embed -> rank -> cite) over an owner-scoped document corpus. Ingest a document (POST /rag/documents) -> it is contained (lone surrogate -> U+FFFD), split into deterministic overlapping CODE-POINT chunks (the central chunking part, vector-proven x3), each embedded by the deterministic offline embedder (the central embedding part); query (POST /rag/query) -> the query is embedded and ranked against the caller's chunks, returning the top-k with a CITATION. The dangerous property is CITATION SOUNDNESS + GROUNDING: every hit carries source {doc_id, start, end} that is an in-bounds window (0 <= start <= end <= len) into the CURRENT stored document, the hit text is exactly that window's code points, and an exact-chunk-text query self-matches at cosine 1.0 -- so an answer is always traceable to a real source chunk and a citation can never be fabricated or stale. Ranking is DETERMINISTIC (score desc, ties by chunk_id asc -- identical x3; scores are floats and deliberately NOT pinned) and a query returns at most k hits (k clamped 1..50). IDENTITY + ISOLATION: both routes are body-only POST mutations requiring an authenticated caller (require_identity; no/invalid token -> 401, PARSE -> AUTH -> SEMANTIC x3). A document is USER-SCOPED two ways: the store key is the composite <owner>\x1f<doc_id> so caller B can NEVER overwrite caller A's doc_id (the cross-owner WRITE wall -- the \x1f separator is a control char well_formed rejects, so the key can't be forged), AND the query scan filters on the authenticated owner FIELD so another owner's chunks are invisible (the cross-corpus RAG-leak wall); the owner is stamped from the token, never a body field, and never surfaced in a hit. The real embedder + vector DB + a smarter splitter swap in behind these routes (INTEROP.md); offline + deterministic + durable.

ratelimit.desc: fixed-window rate limiting: at most LIMIT requests per key per window, the (LIMIT+1)th is 429 · the consume is ONE atomic consume-or-deny read-modify-write through the do seam, so the limit holds even under concurrent processes (a get-then-put limiter is breachable by racing — this one is not) · windows reset cleanly · keys are isolated · the counter is durable (a restart cannot reset a window) · the clock comes from the test-clock seam · strict input validation in all three languages

rbac.desc: access control, deny-by-default, governed by the AUTHENTICATED identity: RBAC (assigned roles -> permissions, fixed policy, least-privilege union) + flat relation tuples (exact (subject, relation, object) match, no wildcard/prefix) · every route requires a bearer token (no token -> 401) · DECISION reads (/can, /check) are CALLER-SCOPED (the subject is the authenticated caller, so a caller asks only about THEIR OWN access) · MUTATIONS (/roles, /relations) are ADMIN-GATED (ARBAC: the caller must hold the 'admin' role, provisioned out-of-band by the operator — no claimable env-name seed; a non-admin -> 403, so no caller can self-escalate) · assigning an unknown role grants NOTHING (201 + allowed:false, never a silent grant) · durable store seam · strict input validation in all three languages

records.desc: records: the App-Layer data substrate — declare a typed record schema, then owner-scoped CRUD (create/list/get/update/delete) over a durable store. Owner-scoped: every record belongs to the caller who created it (the core require_identity seam), the owner is stamped from the authenticated subject NEVER a client field, a by-id get/update/delete of another caller's record is 404 (byte-indistinguishable from missing — existence never leaks cross-owner), and the list returns ONLY the caller's own rows (a stranger gets an empty page, never 403). No mass-assignment: a write reads ONLY the declared field names out of the body's fields map (allowlist-read), so a smuggled owner/id/type (top-level, inside fields, or a case-variant) can never set an authority field. Exactly-once create: the id is derived (deterministic, owner-partitioned, idempotent), so a repeat key returns the SAME record, never a duplicate. Typed validation: each declared field is validated per its type (text/number/boolean/date/datetime/select/json) with cross-language-identical accept/reject, and update is a partial merge of validated declared fields through the atomic read-modify-write seam (never get-then-put) with owner/id/created_at never client-writable. The record type is authored in all three languages (the source of truth); the manifest x-record_schema is a human-readable mirror. Bounded, opaque-cursor pagination; the RFC 9457 problem+json error envelope; durable store seam; strict input validation in all three languages. The keystone every other App-Layer domain builds CRUD on.

reporting.desc: reporting: a self-contained, owner-scoped read-side AGGREGATION store (a CQRS read model the app FEEDS — it does NOT read other domains' stores; the boundary rule forbids a live records join, so the app populates reporting_facts by dual-write / pipeline / outbox, see INTEROP.md). Ingest typed FACT rows (dimensions:{string->string}, measures:{string->strict-integer}) into a named dataset, then POST a GROUP BY rollup (COUNT/SUM/MIN/MAX over the facts, with equality filters, a deterministic order and bounded pagination); GET the raw facts; and DRAIN a dataset. Dangerous properties, all proven x3: (1) AGGREGATION CORRECTNESS - the aggregates are exactly right and byte-identical across python/go/node (a wrong sum still returns 200); (2) OWNER-SCOPED AGGREGATION - an aggregate NEVER includes another owner's facts (the owner is the bearer subject, never a client field, a mandatory conjunct on every scan; a stranger gets an empty result, never 403); (3) DERIVED-OVERFLOW SAFETY - measures are strict integers bounded to +/-(2^53-1) and the SUM predicts overflow BEFORE each add, failing loud with 422 rather than returning a wrapped or precision-lost number; (4) DETERMINISTIC GROUP ORDER - groups are keyed and ordered by the digest of their pre-hashed group-by values, identical x3 regardless of store scan order. Exactly-once, immutable ingest on (owner, dataset, key). every route requires identity; durable across restart; strict input validation in all three languages.

search.desc: token full-text search over a durable corpus: AND-complete and AND-sound (a doc with ALL query terms is returned, a doc missing ANY is not) · whole-token matching only, never substrings · case-insensitive · deny-by-default (an empty query returns [], never the corpus) · DETERMINISTIC ranking: query-term frequency desc, then id asc — reproducible in all three languages · re-indexing an id replaces its document · USER-SCOPED: POST /search/index requires identity (the core require_identity seam — ANY authenticated caller; no/invalid token -> 401) and stamps the doc's owner from the token (never a body field), and GET /search/query ALSO requires identity and returns ONLY the caller's own docs (a non-owner's doc is invisible, the not-yours==not-found pattern; a no-token query is 401) · durable store seam · strict input validation in all three languages

secrets_vault.desc: a versioned secret store (named secrets_vault because 'secrets' collides with Python's stdlib module name): writing a name creates a new IMMUTABLE version (rotation only ever ADDS a version — reveal(N) always returns exactly what was written at N) · a managed version LIFECYCLE — DESTROY irreversibly scrubs a version's bytes (secure_delete), DISABLE/ENABLE reversibly hide/show a version (bytes kept), and SECRETS_VAULT_MAX_VERSIONS prunes the oldest past the cap (the GCP-Secret-Manager / OpenBao state model: enabled|disabled|destroyed, plus pruned — a pruned/destroyed/disabled version is a byte-INDISTINGUISHABLE 404) · the secret value is returned by ONE explicit reveal path only and NEVER by the metadata read, the name list, or the access audit · a domain-local ACCESS AUDIT records every reveal/put/destroy/disable/enable — success AND a 403 denial / 404 probe — to secrets_vault_access (NEVER the value; APP_SECRETS_VAULT_AUDIT off|deny|all; GET /secrets_vault/access, admin) · AT-REST is OPT-IN: set SECRETS_VAULT_KEK (base64 of 32 bytes) and every value is AES-256-GCM SEALED before storage (a fresh per-record 96-bit nonce, name+version AAD so a blob can NOT be replayed under another slot, a self-describing svgcm:keyver:blob); unset = values stored as-is (the honest zero-dependency default, secure_delete scrubs on delete); crypto-shred = destroy the KEK (or a version); a cloud KMS/HSM is the documented INTEROP upgrade · ADMIN-ONLY: EVERY route requires the 'admin' role — no token is 401, a non-admin is 403, resolved BEFORE any body/path validation — existence itself is sensitive in a vault · unknown name/version is 404 · the version counter is bumped atomically per name through the do seam · durable store seam · strict input validation in all three languages

settings.desc: owner-scoped settings with a fixed, typed schema: known keys only (an unknown key is rejected — deny-by-default, no arbitrary keys) · each value is STRICTLY type-checked against its key's declared type (bool/int/string — a string '20' or a boolean true is NOT an int) so a malformed value never reaches the store · reads always return EVERY known key (declared defaults fill the gaps, so a consumer never sees a missing setting) · the owner is the AUTHENTICATED identity (the core require_identity seam) derived from the bearer token, NEVER a path param or caller-supplied owner, so a caller only ever reads/writes THEIR OWN settings · deny-by-default (no token -> 401) · durable store seam · strict input validation in all three languages

storage.desc: object storage behind a swappable provider port (ports-and-adapters), USER-SCOPED: a stored object belongs to its uploader (the core require_identity seam) · every route is deny-by-default authenticated (no token -> 401) · an object is addressed by (owner, key) — the store key is the composite <owner>\x1f<key> so caller A's a.txt and caller B's a.txt are DISTINCT objects (no cross-owner overwrite); the user key is well_formed (no control chars) so the \x1f unit separator can't be forged · the list returns ONLY the caller's own bare keys (owner-filtered) · a cross-owner get/delete is 404, byte-indistinguishable from missing (existence never leaks across owners) · put/get round-trips content byte-for-byte · the etag is CONTENT-ADDRESSED (sha256 of the payload via the digest part — same content, same etag, any change, a different etag) · missing -> 404, delete -> 204 then 404 · the provider is selected ONCE (STORAGE_PROVIDER env): 'store' = the durable runtime store seam (the default AND the deterministic oracle — objects survive a restart), 's3' = a fail-loud customization stub · keys are well-formed identifiers · strict input validation in all three languages

stripe.desc: Stripe-shape payments: charge creation is IDEMPOTENT on the Idempotency-Key, the slot SCOPED TO THE AUTHENTICATED CALLER (digest.scoped_key — an Idempotency-Key is PRIVATE to its caller: caller B can never replay nor 409-grief caller A's charge, the money-domain cross-caller fix) · the claim is ONE atomic read-modify-write through the do seam (no double-charge under racing processes; key reuse with a DIFFERENT body by the SAME caller is 409, matching real Stripe) · the webhook verifies the 'Stripe-Signature: t=,v1=' HMAC over the RAW body via the central signing part, accepting ANY of the comma-listed v1 signatures against ANY active endpoint secret (zero-downtime rotation) inside a replay window from the test-clock seam (tampered payload, forged signature, stale timestamp all 400) · the endpoint secret(s) env-backed, rotatable · durable store seam · strict input validation in all three languages

teams.desc: teams within an org, with role-bearing membership, AUTHORIZED AGAINST ORG MEMBERSHIP: a team is created under exactly one org (the org binding is set once, never changed) · managing a team is an ORG operation — the authenticated caller must be an owner|admin OF THE TEAM'S ORG (the core org_role seam, which reads the orgs_members store orgs writes), so creating a team under an org you don't manage is 403 and only that org's owners/admins add/remove its members (cross-org isolation) · membership is a SET keyed by member handle — adding an existing member UPDATES their role (idempotent upsert, never a duplicate row), removing is idempotent (a non-member is a no-op 200) · the member list is deterministic (sorted by handle) · every mutation is deny-by-default authenticated (no token -> 401), authz resolved BEFORE body validation for the path routes (non-member -> 403 not 422) · cross-team reads of a missing id are an honest 404 · durable store seam · strict input validation in all three languages

tenancy.desc: tenant isolation, the application-level row-scoping shape (models Postgres RLS): every row carries its tenant · EVERY read is scoped to the caller's tenant · cross-tenant access is 404, indistinguishable from missing (never reveals another tenant's row exists) · the tenant is the AUTHENTICATED identity (the core require_identity seam) derived from the bearer token, NEVER a client-supplied X-Tenant-Id header, so a caller cannot read another tenant's rows by setting a header · deny-by-default (no token -> 401) · demo resource: notes under /tenancy · durable store seam · strict input validation in all three languages · the tenant is IMMUTABLE — stamped once at create, never re-homed (no update/delete surface; invariant I8) · the POOL / discriminator model (one shared store, a tenant field re-checked per read — the industry shape: django-multitenant/Hibernate-partition/AWS-pool) · the canonical row-scoping PATTERN reference (the catalog-wide owner-scoping wall keeps every copy honest); the not-yours-is-404 rule is defense-in-depth vs id enumeration (it EXCEEDS the OWASP floor, which is silent on the status code) · multi-user / org-as-tenant is handled by the separate orgs domain

users.desc: user profiles + lifecycle, separate from auth credentials: a handle is claimed EXACTLY ONCE (the claim is atomic through the idempotent_claim part — two processes racing a handle create one user; a duplicate is 409, never a silent overwrite) · profiles are keyed by handle · deactivation is MONOTONIC and idempotent (a terminal-value write; a deactivated user never returns to active) · cross-profile reads of a missing handle are an honest 404 · durable store seam · strict input validation in all three languages · IDENTITY: both mutations require the core require_identity seam (no/invalid token is 401). CREATE is AUTHENTICATED-SELF — a logged-in caller may only mint THEIR OWN handle (handle == caller, else 403), closing handle-squatting/spam. DEACTIVATE is SELF-OR-ADMIN — the account owner OR a core admin (is_admin) may deactivate, anyone else is 403, closing 'anyone permanently kills any account'. The precedence is PARSE -> AUTH(401) -> SEMANTIC(422)/AUTHZ(403), identical in all three languages. GET /{handle} is AUTHENTICATED: a valid session is required (no/invalid token 401) — the profile directory is visible to logged-in callers, not the anonymous public, and any authenticated caller may look up any handle

vectorstore.desc: embeddings + retrieval (the RAG backbone): index documents (text -> a DETERMINISTIC offline embedding) and query for the top-k nearest by cosine · retrieval is GROUNDED (an exact-text query self-matches as the top hit) and DETERMINISTIC (score desc, ties by id asc — identical ordering in all three languages) · a query returns at most k hits · scores are floats and deliberately NOT pinned in the contract (float formatting differs across languages); the contract pins the TOP id and the invariant proves the ordering · re-indexing an id replaces its document · IDENTITY + READ-SCOPING: every mutating route (both POSTs — index and query) requires an authenticated caller via the core require_identity seam (no/invalid token -> 401, any authenticated caller accepted; an unauthenticated otherwise-422 body is 401, never a 422 that leaks shape — PARSE -> AUTH -> SEMANTIC ×3); a document is USER-SCOPED — the OWNER is stamped from the token at index (never a body field) and the query scan returns ONLY the caller's own docs (another owner's doc is skipped, never appearing in hits — the tenancy not-yours==not-found pattern, closing the cross-owner RAG leak); the owner field is internal, never surfaced in the query response · the real embedder + vector DB swap in behind the same routes · durable store seam · strict input validation in all three languages

webhooks.desc: Standard Webhooks signing + verification with MULTI-SECRET ROTATION and INBOUND REPLAY-DEDUP. POST /webhooks/send (ADMIN-ONLY) signs id.ts.payload with EVERY active secret (space-joined) via the central signing part; POST /webhooks/verify (PUBLIC) accepts a delivery matching ANY active secret (rotation never drops a valid webhook), rejects tamper/stale/dotted-id -> invalid, and DEDUPS a replay: a same-id 2nd verify inside the window returns duplicate:true (best-effort exactly-once, the consumer skips it). The dedup write is BEHIND the signature gate (no secret -> no write), scoped to the matched-secret label via digest.scoped_key. Durable msg seq + sent log + seen-set; identical ×3 (WEBHOOK_SECRETS is newline-separated).

pässwörd with späces and 中文 🔑 that is quite long indeed ok

results.content: Let me check that for you.

json.text: lazy dogs sleep peacefully all day long

the quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog
